I don't spend too much time thinking about the world beyond this place—this is where I live, and it's where I belong.

In 1850, "keeping them on task" meant enforcing strict discipline. If a worker was slow or didn’t want to work, the overseers would beat them, or even throw them into the river to make them regret it. The system was built on fear and punishment, with the belief that hard work was a matter of will. If someone didn’t meet expectations, they might be sent to the back of the line, or worse, broken. It was all about control, and the system was designed to keep the workforce in line.
